coredata.dc:title: Efficient performance enhancement of GaN-based vertical light-emitting diodes coated with N-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Deshuai
Li, Hui-Jun
Lyu, Bowen
Cheng, Shiduo
Zhu, Yuankun
Wang, Ping
Wang, Ding
Wang, Xianying
Yang, Junhe
coredata.dc:description: Novel vertical light-emitting diodes (VLEDs) decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been fabricated, based on the metal organic chemical vapor deposition method (MOCVD), wafer bonding technique, laser lift-off (LLO) technique, inductively coupled plasma (ICP) etching and impregnation processing. There is an obvious luminescence enhancement and resistance reduction for VLEDs after coating N-GQDs. The results show an improvement of light output power (LOP), dependent on the improvement of optical extraction efficiency and current spreading. The improvement of external quantum efficiency (EQE) is mainly owing to the roughened surfaces. Meanwhile, an enhancement of electroluminescence (EL) intensity and photoluminescence (PL) intensity has been achieved when the VLEDs were immersed in 0.9 mg/mL of N-GQDs solution, 4 times that of bare VLEDs. After coating N-GQDs, the performance of VLEDs is greatly improved, which could be attributed to the photons recovered by extracting light from waveguide modes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GaN
Vertical light-emitting diodes
N-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic structure and (b) Optical micrograph of the VLEDs.

Characterization of N-GQDs. (a) TEM image; (b) XPS spectra; (c) Raman spectra; and (d) UV-vis spectra and PL spectra of the N-GQDs (the excitation wavelength is 365 nm). The inset in Fig. 2a shows the size distribution obtained from the TEM image.
(a) PL spectra of the VLEDs coated with different concentrations of N-GQDs. (b) PL-enhancement ratio between the VLEDs coated with different concentrations of N-GQDs and the bare VLEDs.

EL spectra of the VLEDs coated with (a) 0 mg/mL, and (b) 0.9 mg/mL of N-GQDs solution, respectively; Injection current dependence of (c) the LOP and (d) the EQE of the VLEDs coated with different concentrations of N-GQDs.
(a) I-V characteristics and (b) WPE curves of the VLEDs coated with different concentrations of N-GQDs; (c) Optical mapping images without (left), and with (right) N-GQDs at 120 mA.
The schematic illustration of the photon recycling effect in VLEDs coated with N-GQDs.
Energy band diagram of the N-GQDs/VLEDs interface under forward bias.

head.ce:title: Efficient performance enhancement of GaN-based vertical light-emitting diodes coated with N-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science & Technology, No. 516 JunGong Road, Shanghai, 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Shanghai for Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Innovation Institute for Materials, Shanghai, 200444, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science & Technology, No. 516 JunGong Road, Shanghai, 200093, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Shanghai for Science & Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel vertical light-emitting diodes (VLEDs) decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been fabricated, based on the metal organic chemical vapor deposition method (MOCVD), wafer bonding technique, laser lift-off (LLO) technique, inductively coupled plasma (ICP) etching and impregnation processing. There is an obvious luminescence enhancement and resistance reduction for VLEDs after coating N-GQDs. The results show an improvement of light output power (LOP), dependent on the improvement of optical extraction efficiency and current spreading. The improvement of external quantum efficiency (EQE) is mainly owing to the roughened surfaces. Meanwhile, an enhancement of electroluminescence (EL) intensity and photoluminescence (PL) intensity has been achieved when the VLEDs were immersed in 0.9 mg/mL of N-GQDs solution, 4 times that of bare VLEDs. After coating N-GQDs, the performance of VLEDs is greatly improved, which could be attributed to the photons recovered by extracting light from waveguide modes.

ce:section.ce:para: GaN-based semiconductors have attracted much attention for its high energy efficiency and low-cost [1–3]. Recently, due to the advanced equipment design, chip processing and improved packaging techniques, the manufacture of GaN-based devices with highly stable light and high-efficiency has been greatly developed [4–6]. Despite of their promising performance, it is necessary to furtherly improve the GaN-based devices of enhanced luminescence and photoconductivity properties via constructing heterojunction structures with other materials [7,8]. Among the various candidates, graphene/Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been investigated intensively in the fields of optoelectronics due to their fascinating electronic and optical properties [9–11]. GQDs (∼3.65 eV) has a lower work function than many conventional semiconductors such as GaAs [12], Si [13], and GaN [14]. Owing to the difference of work functions, photo-induced carriers generated in GQDs could be injected to the semiconductors, resulting in an increase in carrier concentration and current in the semiconductors [15]. Meanwhile, the GQDs could allow hot electron injection under light irradiation due to the presence of proper energy gaps. Their superior luminescence properties have endowed the GQDs promising applications in organic LED devices [16,17]. Herein, N-GQDs prepared via a hydrothermal method have been coated on the VLEDs surface. It was found that there is an obvious enhancement in the LOP and EQE for VLEDs coated with N-GQDs. An enhancement of EL intensity by 25.6% has been achieved when the VLEDs were immersed in 0.9 mg/mL of N-GQDs solution under 120 mA current injection. We also observed that the PL intensity could be enhanced for VLEDs decorated with N-GQDs (0.9 mg/mL), 4 times that of bare VLEDs.

Firstly, GaN-based LEDs epitaxial structures were grown on a sapphire substrate via the metal organic chemical vapor deposition method (MOCVD). The epitaxial structure consisted of a 3 μm of undoped GaN layer, a 2.5 μm of Si-doped n-GaN layer, 5 pairs of multiple quantum-well (MQW) containing 3 nm InGaN wells and 7 nm GaN barriers, and a 650 nm of Mg-doped p-GaN layer. Then the bonding metal layers (Cr/Pt/Au/Sn/Au) were deposited on a p-GaN layer using an e-beam evaporator and the metal layers (Ni/Ti/Pt/Ti/Au) were sequentially deposited on a Si substrate. After that, a wafer bonding technique was used by treating the two components at 320 °C for 5 min to promote the bonding between n-GaN and Si substrate. The total thickness of the bonding metal layer is approximately 1 μm. Furtherly, the sapphire substrates were lapped and polished to achieve a high transparency by a laser lift-off (LLO) technique using a KrF excimer laser (λ max: 248 nm, 750 mJ cm−2), followed by an inductively coupled plasma (ICP) etching and wet etching process to remove the UID-GaN layer. Finally, a Ni/Au metal layer was utilized as the n-metal contact [18,19]. The nitrogen doped GQDs were synthesized via a traditional hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as the precursors [20,21]. The precursors were dissolved in 50 ml of water and the mixed solution was reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained GQDs was washed with ethanol and centrifuged repeatedly for three to five times. The final solids could be easily dispersed in water. After that, the obtained VLEDs were immersed in the N-GQDs aqueous solutions (0–1.5 mg/mL) for 30 min, followed by drying at 100 °C for 30 min. The schematic structure and optical micrograph of the VLEDs is shown in Fig. 1 .

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope. The current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of VLEDs was measured by a semiconductor characterization system (Keithley 4200). The light output power and external quantum efficiency (EQE) were measured by using a calibrated integrating sphere (ATA-500, Everfine Corporation, China). Optical mapping images was measured using an SRC-600 spectral radiance colorimeter (Everfine Corporation, China).

Fig. 2 (a) shows the TEM image of N-GQDs. Based on the analysis of size distribution seen from the inset in Fig. 2(a), the average lateral size of the N-GQDs is 3.0 ± 0.5 nm. The size distribution of N-GQDs was measured using the imaging software (Image J) across the diameter of the particles. The XPS survey spectrum of the N-GQDs in Fig. 2(b) shows a predominant C 1s speak centered at 284.5 eV, O 1s peak at 531.4 eV and N 1s peak at 499.6 eV. The XPS results show N-GQDs have been successfully synthesized. Raman spectroscopy was performed to detect the graphite structure of the material. Raman spectra of all N-GQDs show an obvious D band at 1348 cm−1 and a bit stronger G band at 1597 cm−1 (Fig. 2(c)), indicative of well-crystalline graphite structure [22–24]. As seen from Fig. 2(f), the N-GQDs exhibited an obvious π–π* transition of CC domain, and the n–π* transition of CN & CN conjugated bonds at 350 nm [25]. The PL spectra of the N-GQDs exhibited a main peak position at 442 nm, corresponding to the typical emission originated from π→π* transition [26].

The PL spectra of the GaN-based VLEDs after being immersed in different concentrations of N-GQDs are depicted in Fig. 3 . Owing to the band-edge emission of GaN, the spectra all show emission peaks at 367 nm. From 0 to 0.9 mg/mL, the PL intensity of VLEDs increases with the increase of N-GQDs concentration, and then decrease when the concentration of N-GQDs is higher. The maximum PL intensity reveals an enhancement factor of 25% compared to that of bare VLEDs, indicating increasing recombined carriers in the VLEDs after introducing N-GQDs. Fig. 3(b) shows the PL-enhancement ratio between the VLEDs coated with different concentrations of N-GQDs and the bare VLEDs. It could be found that the PL-enhancement ratio increases of VLEDs firstly and then decreases by increasing the concentration of N-GQDs. The ratio achieved the highest when the concentration of N-GQDs is 0.9 mg/mL, about 5.5 times.

The EL spectra of the InGaN/GaN VLEDs coated without and with N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b), respectively. The main EL peak can be observed at about 450 nm, which corresponds to the emission of InGaN/GaN MQW in the active layer. After introducing N-GQDs, the EL intensity of the VLEDs enhanced by 25.6% have been achieved after being immersed in 0.9 mg/ml of N-GQDs solution, compared to that of bare VLEDs. The light output power (LOP) related to the injection current for different VLEDs is shown in Fig. 4(c). Similarly, the VLEDs coated with N-GQDs exhibited a 25% improvement in LOP, compared to that of bare VLEDs. It is shown that the light output reached the maximum when the N-GQDs concentration is 0.9 mg/mL and then decreased, probably resulting from the shielding effect of the thickened N-GQDs layer. Since the anti-reflection film could increase the critical angle, the Fresnel reflection decreases [27]. Fig. 4(d) displays the EQE curves for different injection current. EQE can be calculated as [28]:
EQE =
P
opt
. e
h ⋅ v ⋅ I

The P
opt , h, v, I and e, respectively, describe optical power, Planck constant, light frequency, the injection current and the electron charge. As a result, it could be found that the EQE exhibited the highest value when the concentration of N-GQDs is 0.9 mg/mL. Our device exhibited a 25.6% improvement in EQE when the concentration of N-GQDs is 0.9 mg/mL, compare to the bare VLEDs. In our work, the deposition of N-GQDs on the surface of VLEDs would not change the composition and geometry of the active layers. The enhancement of EQE largely depends on the light extraction efficiency which is related to the structural factors of V-LEDs such as its surface texture. Therefore, the improvements of EQE are mainly attributed to the roughened surface of the V-LEDs after coating N-GQDs, while over deposition of N-GQDs could lead to a decrease of EQE via hampering the light output.

Fig. 5 (a) displays that the current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of the VLEDs without and with N-GQDs decoration. It was found that the current level at a specific voltage was enhanced with the increase of N-GQDs concentrations. This is beneficial to the LED performance, indicating that the existence of N-GQDs was expected to spread current more uniformly. Fig. 5(b) shows the wall-plug efficiency (WPE) for different injection current. Obviously, the WPE of VLEDs increases when the concentration of N-GQDs increase from 0 to 1.5 mg/mL. To investigate the light emission mapping images, Fig. 5(c) shows the VLEDs coated without (Left) and with (Right) N-GQDs (0.9 mg/mL) under 120 mA current injection. It is obvious that the light emission intensity of the VLEDs-NGQDs device is greatly improved under the same testing condition. When the N-GQDs on the surface of VLEDs is irradiated by EL, photo-electrons can be generated in N-GQDs which will be transferred to the active layer of LEDs through the GaN layer at the top. The transfer of these carriers (electrons) results in the transverse expansion of the carriers, which contributes to improve the current spreading, and reduce the resistance in the VLEDs.

Based on above discussions, Fig. 6 shows the light extraction scheme in GaN-based VLEDs with/without coating of N-GQDs. It is well known that total internal reflections form at the air/semiconductor interface. When N-GQDs is coated on the surface of VLEDs, evanescent field interacts with N-GQDs, and N-GQDs can extract evanescent light energy.

The energy band diagram of the N-GQDs/VLEDs is shown in Fig. 7 . With the addition of a forward bias, the generated hot electrons of N-GQDs was injected into the conduction band of gallium nitride through the tunnel and then moved to the active layer in InGaN/GaN MQW. The injected electrons can recombine with holes, producing an enhanced electroluminescence. Therefore, due to the introduction of N-GQDs on the VLEDs surface, electron injection from N-GQDs could efficiently improve the light output power. Because the work function of N-GQDs is lower than that of GaN, the photo-induced carriers could be efficiently transferred from N-GQDs to GaN through the N-GQDs/GaN interface.

We have demonstrated a novel method to significantly improve the electrical and optical performance of VLEDs via coating N-GQDs on the surface of GaN-based VLEDs. After being immersed in 0.9 mg/ml of N-GQDs solution, both LOP and EQE of the VLEDs coated with N-GQDs were increased, 4 times that of pure VLEDs. Compared to bare VLEDs, the performance enhancement of the VLEDs coated with N-GQDs was supposed to be attributed to the photons which are recovered by extracting light from the waveguide modes. Due to appropriate difference of work functions, the photo-generated electrons in N-GQDs could be efficiently transferred into the active layer of VLEDs, thus leading to improvement of optical and electrical properties.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51572173, 51602197, 51771121 and 51702212), Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission (16060502300, 16JC402200 and 18511110600), Shanghai Eastern Scholar Program (QD2016014).
